skill.name: competitor-profiling
skill.description: Research competitor websites from user-provided URLs and create structured markdown profiles with positioning, pricing, SEO, customer, and market signals.
skill.summary: Build structured competitor profiles from websites, SEO data, and review sources.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: Static high-severity weak-cryptography detections are false positives from markdown and business terminology, and the repeated backtick execution detections are markdown code fences. The skill is not malicious, but it intentionally uses network-based research tools and saves raw scraped data to disk, so publication should include a data handling warning.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Competitor Research via Third-Party Tools
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to map and scrape competitor sites with Firecrawl and collect SEO and market data with DataForSEO. This is core functionality, but it sends user-provided competitor URLs and domains to external services and may collect third-party website content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly call Firecrawl and DataForSEO workflows on competitor domains. The behavior is legitimate for the skill, but the network and data-sharing risk is direct.

medium_findings.title: Raw Scrape and SEO Data Written to Project Files
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to persist raw scrape, SEO, and review data under competitor-profiles before synthesis. This improves auditability, but it can leave copies of third-party content, review text, and market data in the user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file persistence instructions are repeated and specific, including paths and file types. The risk is data retention rather than malicious file access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Detections Are Benign Eval Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs detected in the eval file are placeholder competitor URLs inside a test prompt. They are not executable network calls and do not direct data to a suspicious endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 6 contains sample input for an evaluation case, not code. No credential transfer or hidden callback behavior is present there.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Detections Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in markdown examples, file path placeholders, and template fences. No executable script or command runner is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks delimit markdown code blocks or inline path names. I found no Ruby code, shell execution primitive, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts point to ordinary competitor-profile text, pricing/profile template content, or SEO terminology. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or password hashing operation is implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown prose and JSON evaluation text, not cryptographic code. The surrounding context confirms ordinary market research terminology.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Research Guidance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts point to profile fields such as target audience and tool guidance for detecting competitor technology stacks. This is market research context, not host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines discuss competitor website analysis and crawl limits. They do not inspect the local system, enumerate processes, or gather host environment details.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Website Mapping and Scraping
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow uses Firecrawl map, scrape, search, crawl, and extract operations against competitor websites and review sources. Users should confirm they are allowed to collect and store the target content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool usage is explicitly described and repeated across the main instructions and reference file. The risk depends on user authorization and target site terms.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Raw Data Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow stores raw scrape, SEO, and review outputs in dated local folders before producing final profiles. This can retain sensitive market research or copied third-party material longer than expected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions specify exact output folders and raw data categories. This is intentional data persistence rather than accidental logging.

content.value_statement: Competitive research often becomes inconsistent when teams collect pages, SEO metrics, and notes by hand. This skill creates repeatable competitor profiles from URLs, with raw sources saved for review.
actual_capabilities: Maps competitor websites and identifies pages for pricing, features, customers, integrations, and product updates.
Scrapes selected pages and stores raw markdown or JSON in dated project folders.
Collects SEO and backlink metrics using DataForSEO tool references when available.
Builds structured markdown profiles with positioning, pricing, customer proof, SEO, strengths, and weaknesses.
Creates a cross-competitor summary with comparison tables, positioning maps, takeaways, and market gaps.
Supports quick scans, deep profiles, and profile updates with change logs.
limitations: It depends on external Firecrawl and DataForSEO tools for live data collection.
It cannot verify private company metrics unless reliable public sources are available.
Scraped data can be blocked, stale, incomplete, or restricted by target site terms.
Large competitor lists should be prioritized because deep profiles can be slow and costly.

use_cases.title: Prepare Product Positioning Research
use_cases.description: Create consistent competitor profiles before a messaging, pricing, or positioning review.

use_cases.title: Support Sales Enablement Planning
use_cases.description: Collect competitor strengths, weaknesses, pricing signals, and customer proof before building battle cards.

use_cases.title: Find SEO and Content Gaps
use_cases.description: Compare competitor traffic, ranking pages, backlink signals, and content focus areas.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Competitor Scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one or two competitors.
prompt_templates.prompt: Profile these competitors with a quick scan: [URLs]. Focus on positioning, pricing, and the strongest public proof points.

prompt_templates.title: Deep Competitive Profile
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed research for a small competitor set.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create deep profiles for these competitors: [URLs]. Include site content, pricing, customers, reviews, SEO metrics, backlinks, and product direction signals.

prompt_templates.title: Update Existing Profiles
prompt_templates.scenario: Refresh prior competitor research without losing history.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing profiles for [competitors]. Re-check pricing, SEO metrics, changelog updates, and add a change log for each profile.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Against Our Product
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis using internal positioning context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Profile these competitors against our product context in .agents/product-marketing.md. Highlight market gaps, threats, and positioning opportunities.

output_examples.input: Profile three SaaS competitors for a positioning review.
output_examples.output: The skill produces one markdown profile per competitor, plus a summary with side-by-side metrics, positioning themes, opportunities, and threats.
The skill rechecks pricing, SEO metrics, and product updates, then adds a dated change log while preserving previous raw data folders.

output_examples.input: Skip SEO data and focus on pricing and customer proof.
output_examples.output: The skill creates an abbreviated profile and marks SEO sections as not collected by request instead of leaving placeholders.

best_practices: Provide your product context before asking for competitive implications.
Start with quick scans for large lists, then deepen the highest-priority competitors.
Review raw source folders before using findings in customer-facing material.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to scrape sites when you lack permission or a valid business reason.
Do not treat customer logo walls or marketing claims as verified facts without cross-checking.
Do not run deep profiles for many competitors before narrowing the list.

faq.question: What input does this skill need?
faq.answer: It needs competitor URLs, your product context, desired depth, and any priority research areas.

faq.question: Does it create final files?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to create markdown profiles and a summary under competitor-profiles.

faq.question: Does it require external tools?
faq.answer: Yes. Live scraping and SEO metrics depend on Firecrawl and DataForSEO MCP tools.

faq.question: Can it skip SEO research?
faq.answer: Yes. It can focus on positioning, pricing, product pages, and customer proof when requested.

faq.question: How does it handle many competitors?
faq.answer: It recommends quick scans and prioritization when the list is large, usually starting with the top five.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The workflow sends target URLs to external research tools and stores raw scraped data locally.
